steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for a point that the author of Text 1 would most likely agree with from Text 2. That means the answer must be a claim both texts actually make or both imply — not something only Text 2 claims, and not something Text 1 would reject.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Capital and Ideology is notably different in structure from some of Piketty's earlier work': Text 1 explicitly contrasts the "more rigorous structure" of the first book with the second book's digressive chapters. Text 2 opens by stating the book "has different aims than Piketty's earlier books." Both texts agree on this structural difference, even though they evaluate it oppositely. ✓
  • Choice B — 'The material on vegetarian diets in India is essential to the book': Text 1 specifically cites that material as an example of tedious digressions that muddy the reasoning. The author of Text 1 would not agree. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Capital in the Twenty-First Century is a superior book': Text 2 never concedes superiority; it argues the second book should be judged on its own terms. The author of Text 2 would reject this claim, so there is no agreement. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Capital and Ideology was influenced by Robert Burton': Text 2 uses Burton only as an analogy, not as a claim of influence. Text 1 does not mention Burton at all. Neither text makes this claim. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
